1:
"The former treatise have I made, O Theophilus, of all that Jesus began both to do and teach," The witnesses note the prologue of Acts, its link to Luke's Gospel, and the force of "began." Chrysostom commends the book as little known yet rich: "it may profit us no less than even the Gospels, so replete is it with Christian wisdom and sound doctrine, especially in what is said concerning the Holy Ghost"; in it "the predictions which in the Gospels Christ utters, here we may see actually come to pass," and "the mighty change taking place in the disciples now that the Spirit has come upon them." The witnesses note the "former treatise" is Luke's Gospel, addressed to Theophilus; and the pregnant word "began" — Jesus "began to do and teach," the Acts continuing his work.

The witnesses note the prologue: the Gospel records what Jesus began to do; the Acts, what he continues by the Spirit.

So the witnesses make the verse the prologue and the link to the Gospel. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Acts continues the work of Christ — the Gospel recording what he began, the Acts what he carries on by his Spirit through the apostles. The witnesses note the reference: "the former treatise" is Luke's Gospel, the first of the two books addressed to Theophilus; and the writer now takes up the story where the Gospel left off, at the ascension. The expositors press the force of the word "began": Luke writes that the Gospel told "all that Jesus began both to do and teach" — implying that the Acts records what Jesus continued to do and teach; his work was not ended at the ascension, but went on, now by his Spirit through the apostles. The witnesses draw out this rich truth: the Gospel is the account of what Christ did in the flesh; the Acts is the account of what the risen, ascended Christ continues to do from heaven, through the Spirit and the Church — so that the acts of the apostles are, in truth, the continuing acts of the living Christ. The expositors observe Chrysostom's commendation of the book: little known, yet as profitable as the Gospels, full of Christian wisdom and sound doctrine, especially concerning the Holy Ghost; in it the predictions of the Gospels are seen fulfilled, and the transformation of the disciples by the Spirit displayed — the timid become bold, the weak made mighty witnesses. The witnesses note the unity of the two books: the Gospel and the Acts are one continuous history — the work of Christ begun in his earthly ministry and continued in his heavenly reign; the same Lord, the same Spirit, the same gospel, advancing from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth. The expositors draw the lesson on the living Christ: he who began to do and teach is still doing and teaching, by his Spirit, in his Church; the Acts the proof that Christ lives and works, and that his cause goes forward by his power. The witnesses draw the application: the believer reads the Acts as the continuing work of the living Christ, and takes courage that the Lord who began his work carries it on to this day. The verse thus opens the Acts with the prologue — what Jesus began to do and teach — teaching that the book continues the work of Christ, now by his Spirit through the apostles, the same Lord advancing his gospel from heaven.
2:
"Until the day in which he was taken up, after that he through the Holy Ghost had given commandments unto the apostles whom he had chosen:" The witnesses note the close of the Gospel at the ascension and the syntax of "through the Holy Ghost." Gill notes the former treatise reached "until the day in which he was taken up... into heaven." Lightfoot discusses where "through the Holy Ghost" belongs: whether with "the apostles whom he had chosen through the Holy Ghost," or (as the Greek order and most translations rightly render) "after that he through the Holy Ghost had given commandments" — Christ giving his commands by the Spirit, as God of old dictated to the prophets by his Spirit. The witnesses note Christ's commandments to the chosen apostles, given through the Spirit, before his ascension.

The witnesses note Christ's parting commands — given through the Holy Ghost — to the apostles he had chosen.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's commands to the apostles before his ascension. The lesson the expositors draw is that the risen Christ instructed his chosen apostles by the Spirit, the same Spirit who would empower their mission. The witnesses note the marker of time: "until the day in which he was taken up" — the Gospel reached to the ascension, and now the Acts takes up from there; the ascension the hinge between the two books, the close of Christ's earthly work and the opening of his heavenly. The expositors observe the syntax that Lightfoot weighs: the phrase "through the Holy Ghost" may grammatically attach either to the choosing of the apostles or to the giving of commandments; and Lightfoot, with the Greek order and most translations, takes it with the commandments — "he through the Holy Ghost had given commandments." The witnesses draw out the meaning: Christ gave his parting instructions to the apostles by the Holy Ghost, his human nature anointed with the Spirit without measure; as God of old spoke to the prophets by his Spirit, so the risen Christ commanded his apostles by the same Spirit — the commands of Christ being the words of the Spirit. The expositors note the chosen apostles: "the apostles whom he had chosen" — the eleven (Judas now fallen), selected by Christ himself, to whom he committed his commands and his mission; the foundation of the Church, chosen and instructed by the Lord. The witnesses press the connection with what follows: the commands given by the Spirit prepare for the promise of the Spirit (v.5) — the apostles, instructed by the Spirit, are to wait for the Spirit's power; the whole mission of the Church a work of the Holy Ghost, from the commands of Christ to the witness of the apostles. The expositors observe the authority of the apostolic commission: the commands the apostles received were not their own, but Christ's, given by the Spirit; their mission grounded in the authority of the risen Lord. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks that the work of the Church proceeds from the commands of the risen Christ, given by the Spirit; and that the same Spirit who instructed the apostles empowers the gospel still. The verse thus records Christ's commands to the apostles — given through the Holy Ghost before his ascension — teaching that the risen Lord instructed his chosen apostles by the Spirit, grounding their mission in his authority and the Spirit's power.
3:
"To whom also he shewed himself alive after his passion by many infallible proofs, being seen of them forty days, and speaking of the things pertaining to the kingdom of God:" The witnesses note the proofs of the resurrection and the forty days' teaching. Gill stresses the certainty of his death (acknowledged even by the Jews, confirmed by the pierced side, the centurion, the burial) and then the certainty of his risen life: "he raised himself from the dead, and hereby declared himself to be the Son of God with power." The witnesses note the "many infallible proofs" — repeated, undeniable appearances over forty days — and that Christ used the time to teach "the things pertaining to the kingdom of God." The witnesses note the resurrection established beyond doubt and the kingdom expounded.

The witnesses note the risen Christ proving his life by many infallible proofs over forty days, teaching the kingdom.

So the witnesses make the verse the proofs of the resurrection and the forty days' instruction. The lesson the expositors draw is the certainty of the resurrection, established by many infallible proofs, and the use Christ made of the forty days in teaching the kingdom. The witnesses note the foundation laid: "he shewed himself alive after his passion by many infallible proofs" — the resurrection proved not by a single doubtful sighting, but by many certain, undeniable evidences; the risen Christ seen, handled, eating, conversing, over and again. The expositors press the certainty Gill establishes on both sides: first the certainty of his death — his passion real, his death confirmed by the pierced side, the centurion's report, the burial; and then the certainty of his risen life — he showed himself alive, raising himself from the dead, declared thereby the Son of God with power (Romans 1:4). The witnesses observe the force of "infallible proofs": the evidences were such as could not deceive — repeated appearances to many witnesses, over a period of forty days, with every test of sight and touch and ordinary intercourse; the resurrection the best-attested fact of history, proved beyond all reasonable doubt. The expositors draw the lesson on the foundation of the faith: the gospel rests on the certainty of the resurrection, and the resurrection on the many infallible proofs Christ gave; the faith is no fable, but founded on the attested fact of the risen Lord. The witnesses note the forty days: Christ remained forty days before ascending, appearing at intervals, "speaking of the things pertaining to the kingdom of God" — using the time to instruct the apostles in the nature, the laws, the mysteries of his kingdom; preparing them, by his teaching, for their mission. The expositors observe the subject of his teaching: the kingdom of God — not an earthly kingdom (as the disciples still hoped, v.6), but the spiritual reign of grace, the gospel kingdom the apostles would proclaim; Christ grounding them in its truth before he left. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rests his faith on the resurrection, proved by many infallible proofs; and values the kingdom-teaching of the risen Christ, the foundation of the apostolic gospel. The verse thus records the proofs of the resurrection and the forty days' teaching — many infallible proofs, the kingdom expounded — teaching the certainty of the risen Christ and the instruction by which he prepared his apostles for their mission.
4:
"And, being assembled together with them, commanded them that they should not depart from Jerusalem, but wait for the promise of the Father, which, saith he, ye have heard of me." The witnesses note Christ's charge to wait at Jerusalem for the promised Spirit. Lightfoot examines the word "assembled together" (synalizomenos), deriving it not from "salt" but from "assembly" (a gathering) — Christ gathering them; though some render it "eating with them." Gill takes this as the last meeting at Bethany or Olivet. The witnesses note Christ commanding the apostles not to leave Jerusalem, but to wait for "the promise of the Father" — the Holy Spirit, which they had heard him promise. The witnesses note the charge to wait for the Spirit at Jerusalem.

The witnesses note Christ's charge: not to depart Jerusalem, but to wait for the promised Spirit.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's charge to wait for the Spirit. The lesson the expositors draw is the duty of waiting for the promised power before the work, and the certainty of the Father's promise. The witnesses note the setting: "being assembled together with them" — Christ gathered with the apostles (Lightfoot derives the word from "assembly," a gathering, though some render "eating with them," another proof of his risen life); the last meeting before the ascension. The expositors note the command: "that they should not depart from Jerusalem, but wait for the promise of the Father" — the apostles, eager perhaps to begin their work or return to Galilee, are charged to stay at Jerusalem and wait; not to act until the promised Spirit came. The witnesses press the meaning of "the promise of the Father": the gift of the Holy Spirit, promised by the Father (Joel 2:28; and by Christ, John 14-16), to be poured out on the Church; called the Father's promise because the Father promised and would send him. The expositors draw the lesson on waiting for the Spirit: the apostles, though commissioned and instructed, were not to begin until endued with power from on high (Luke 24:49); the work of the gospel must be done in the Spirit's power, not in human strength, and so they must wait for the promised gift. The witnesses observe the discipline of waiting: it is hard to wait when one is eager to work; yet Christ commands it — for the work done before the Spirit comes is fruitless, and the power must precede the witness; the waiting itself an act of obedience and faith. The expositors note the certainty of the promise: "which ye have heard of me" — Christ had repeatedly promised the Spirit, and now confirms it; the apostles wait not in uncertainty but on a sure promise, knowing the Spirit would surely come. The witnesses draw the application: the believer learns to wait for the Spirit's power before the work, to do nothing for Christ in mere natural strength, and to rest on the certainty of the Father's promise; the waiting the path to the power. The verse thus records Christ's charge to wait at Jerusalem for the Spirit — the promise of the Father — teaching the duty of waiting for the promised power, the certainty of the promise, and the dependence on the Spirit for all true work.
5:
"For John truly baptized with water; but ye shall be baptized with the Holy Ghost not many days hence." The witnesses note the contrast of John's water baptism with the baptism of the Spirit. Gill notes John's baptism was "water baptism, an immersion of persons in water," John the first administrator; and these are "the words of Christ himself," heard from his own mouth, not recorded by the evangelists. Lightfoot stresses the force of "not many days hence" (ou meta pollas tautas hēmeras) — pointing to the nearness of Pentecost. The witnesses note Christ promising the baptism of the Spirit, distinct from and surpassing John's baptism in water, soon to come. The witnesses note the Spirit-baptism near at hand.

The witnesses note the contrast: John baptized with water, but the apostles would soon be baptized with the Spirit.

So the witnesses make the verse the promise of the baptism of the Spirit. The lesson the expositors draw is the surpassing of John's water baptism by the baptism of the Holy Ghost, and the nearness of its coming. The witnesses note the contrast: "John truly baptized with water; but ye shall be baptized with the Holy Ghost" — John's baptism (Gill notes, an immersion in water, John the first to administer it) was the baptism of preparation, pointing forward; but the apostles would receive a greater baptism, the baptism of the Holy Ghost, the outpouring of the Spirit at Pentecost. The expositors press the difference: John baptized with the outward element of water, a sign of repentance; but Christ would baptize with the Holy Ghost, the inward and powerful gift, filling, empowering, transforming — the substance to which the water pointed. The witnesses observe that these are Christ's own words, heard from his mouth (Gill notes, not recorded by the evangelists but preserved here by Luke) — repeating the promise John himself had made ("he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost," Matthew 3:11); the promise now to be fulfilled. The expositors draw the lesson on the baptism of the Spirit: the Pentecostal outpouring would be the baptism Christ promised — the Spirit poured out on the Church, empowering the apostles for their witness, the inward and mighty work surpassing all outward rites. The witnesses note the nearness Lightfoot marks in "not many days hence": the promise was not for a distant time, but for a few days — ten days later, at Pentecost; the apostles were to wait, but not long, for the speedy coming of the Spirit. The expositors observe the encouragement in the nearness: the apostles' waiting would soon be rewarded; the promised power was close at hand, the baptism of the Spirit imminent. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the surpassing gift of the Spirit, foreshadowed by water but fulfilled in the baptism of the Holy Ghost; and rests on the certainty and nearness of God's promises. The verse thus records the promise of the baptism of the Spirit — surpassing John's water, not many days hence — teaching the greater baptism of the Holy Ghost and the nearness of the Pentecostal gift.
6:
"When they therefore were come together, they asked of him, saying, Lord, wilt thou at this time restore again the kingdom to Israel?" The witnesses note the disciples' lingering hope of an earthly kingdom. Chrysostom observes their motive: "they were quite ready for freedom, for they had no mind to address themselves to danger; what they wished was to breathe freely again" after the perils they had endured; and they asked "not when? but whether at this time" — eager for the restoration. The witnesses note that the apostles, even now, still looked for a temporal kingdom restored to Israel — a misunderstanding the risen Christ would correct. The witnesses note the disciples' question revealing their earthly expectation.

The witnesses note the disciples still hoping for an earthly kingdom — asking if Christ would now restore it to Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' lingering earthly hope. The lesson the expositors draw is the persistence of their misunderstanding, expecting a temporal kingdom even after the resurrection, and the gentleness with which Christ would correct it. The witnesses note the question: "Lord, wilt thou at this time restore again the kingdom to Israel?" — the apostles, despite all Christ's teaching, still cherished the hope of an earthly, national kingdom, the throne of David restored, Israel freed from Rome and exalted among the nations; the deep-rooted expectation of their people. The expositors observe Chrysostom's reading of their state: they were eager for freedom and relief, having endured great peril; they longed to "breathe freely again," to see the kingdom restored and their dangers past; and they asked not "when" but "whether at this time" — hoping the moment had come. The witnesses press the persistence of the misunderstanding: even after the cross, the resurrection, and forty days of kingdom-teaching, the apostles still thought in earthly terms — slow to grasp that Christ's kingdom was spiritual, not national; not the restoration of Israel's throne, but the reign of grace in the hearts of all nations. The expositors draw the lesson on the slowness of even good men to lay aside cherished but mistaken hopes: the apostles' error was not malice but the deep grip of national expectation; and Christ would correct it not by harsh rebuke but by redirecting them (the verses to come) — from speculation about times to the mission of witness. The witnesses note the contrast with what Christ would give: they asked for an earthly kingdom restored to Israel; he would give them the Spirit's power and a witness to all nations — a kingdom far greater than they imagined, spiritual and universal. The expositors observe the patience of Christ: he does not scold their dullness, but gently turns them from their mistaken question to their true calling. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is warned against the persistence of earthly expectations of Christ's kingdom, and is to seek the spiritual reign Christ came to establish, not the temporal glory the flesh desires. The verse thus records the disciples' lingering earthly hope — wilt thou restore the kingdom to Israel? — teaching the persistence of their misunderstanding and the spiritual kingdom Christ would set them to proclaim.
7:
"And he said unto them, It is not for you to know the times or the seasons, which the Father hath put in his own power." The witnesses note Christ's check on vain curiosity about times. Barnes explains the answer "meets precisely their inquiry": their question was about the time of the restoration, and Christ tells them the time of the great events of God's kingdom was not for them to know — as he had said before, "of that day and that hour knoweth no man" (Matthew 24:36); "God has uniformly reproved a vain curiosity on such points" (1 Thessalonians 5:1; 2 Peter 3:10). Barnes notes the distinction: "times" any indefinite period, "seasons" a fixed, appropriate time. The witnesses note Christ withholding the knowledge of times, reserved to the Father. The witnesses note the check on curiosity about times.

The witnesses note Christ's check: the times and seasons are reserved to the Father, not given the disciples to know.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's check on curiosity about times. The lesson the expositors draw is that the times of God's purposes are reserved to the Father, and vain curiosity about them is not for the believer. The witnesses note the answer: "It is not for you to know the times or the seasons" — Christ does not gratify their curiosity about when the kingdom would come, but tells them plainly that the knowledge of such times is not given them; the time of the great events of God's kingdom is hidden. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the distinction: "times" denotes any indefinite period, "seasons" a fixed, appropriate time; the whole scheme of God's appointed periods and crises is meant — and all of it withheld from the disciples' knowledge. The witnesses press the reservation: "which the Father hath put in his own power" — the times are kept in the Father's own authority, reserved to his sovereign will, not revealed to men nor even (in his human knowledge) to the Son to declare; a matter of the Father's secret counsel. The expositors draw the lesson on vain curiosity: God has uniformly reproved the curiosity that would pry into the times of his purposes — the day of the Lord comes as a thief, the times not for us to know (1 Thessalonians 5:1-2; 2 Peter 3:10); the believer is not to speculate about dates and seasons, but to attend to his duty. The witnesses observe the redirection Christ makes: instead of feeding their curiosity about times, he turns them (the next verse) to their true business — to receive the Spirit and be witnesses; the cure for vain speculation is faithful service. The expositors note the wisdom of the reservation: God hides the times for good reason — that his people may live in constant readiness, watching always, neither presuming on delay nor despairing of fulfilment; the hidden times keeping the Church watchful. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to cease from curious speculation about the times of God's purposes, to leave them in the Father's hand, and to attend to the work and watchfulness God has given; the times are his, the duty ours. The verse thus records Christ's check on curiosity about times — reserved to the Father — teaching that the times of God's purposes are his secret, vain curiosity reproved, and the believer turned from speculation to faithful service.
8:
"But ye shall receive power, after that the Holy Ghost is come upon you: and ye shall be witnesses unto me both in Jerusalem, and in all Judaea, and in Samaria, and unto the uttermost part of the earth." The witnesses note the promise of power and the program of the apostolic mission. Gill notes the power was "the power of the Holy Ghost, strength from him to preach the Gospel, and work miracles... courage and greatness of mind amidst all reproaches and persecutions" — not the worldly ease they sought, but service and trial. The witnesses note the threefold gift: the Spirit's power, the office of witness, and the expanding field — Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, the ends of the earth; the very outline of the book of Acts. The witnesses note the program of the gospel's advance.

The witnesses note the promise: the Spirit's power, the witness to Christ, and the mission expanding to the ends of the earth.

So the witnesses make the verse the promise of power and the program of the mission. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Spirit's power equips the apostles to be Christ's witnesses, and that the gospel is to advance from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth. The witnesses note the redirection: instead of the times they asked about, Christ gives them their true business — "ye shall receive power, after that the Holy Ghost is come upon you"; not knowledge of dates, but power for witness. The expositors press the nature of the power Gill names: the power of the Holy Ghost — strength to preach the gospel, to work miracles, and above all courage and greatness of mind to face reproach and persecution, to confess Christ and stand for him; not the worldly ease and freedom they had sought, but the power to serve and suffer. The witnesses note the office: "ye shall be witnesses unto me" — the apostles to testify of Christ, of his life, death, resurrection, and gospel; the Spirit's power given for this end, that they might bear effective witness to the Lord. The expositors draw out the program of the mission: "in Jerusalem, and in all Judaea, and in Samaria, and unto the uttermost part of the earth" — the very outline of the book of Acts: beginning at Jerusalem (chs. 1-7), spreading through Judea and Samaria (chs. 8-12), and reaching to the ends of the earth (chs. 13-28); the gospel advancing in widening circles from the center to the world. The witnesses press the universality of the commission: the gospel is for all nations, not the Jews only; the witness to Christ to go out from Jerusalem to the uttermost parts, the whole earth its field — the great commission in its geographic unfolding. The expositors observe the contrast with the disciples' question: they asked for a kingdom restored to Israel; Christ gives them a mission to the world — a kingdom far greater, spiritual and universal, advancing by the Spirit's power through their witness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer receives the Spirit's power for witness, is called to testify of Christ, and shares in the mission that reaches to the ends of the earth; the program of Acts the pattern for the Church's mission still. The verse thus records the promise of power and the program of the mission — the Spirit, the witness, the expanding field — teaching the Spirit's empowering, the office of witness, and the gospel's advance from Jerusalem to the uttermost part of the earth.
9:
"And when he had spoken these things, while they beheld, he was taken up; and a cloud received him out of their sight." The witnesses note the visible ascension of Christ. Barnes stresses the importance of the disciples seeing it: "it is not affirmed in the New Testament that they saw him rise from the dead, because the evidence of that fact could be better established by their seeing him after he was risen; but the truth of his ascension could not be confirmed in that manner — hence it was so arranged that he should ascend in open day," visibly, while they watched. The witnesses note Christ taken up bodily before their eyes, a cloud receiving him — the visible, attested ascension. The witnesses note the ascension witnessed by the apostles.

The witnesses note the visible ascension — Christ taken up before the apostles' eyes, a cloud receiving him.

So the witnesses make the verse the visible ascension of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the importance of the ascension being seen and attested, the bodily return of Christ to heaven before his disciples' eyes. The witnesses note the manner: "while they beheld, he was taken up" — Christ ascended visibly, in the sight of the apostles, who watched him rise; not secretly, but openly, that they might be eyewitnesses of his going to heaven. The expositors press Barnes's observation on the wisdom of this: the New Testament does not say the disciples saw Christ rise from the dead — for the resurrection was better proved by their seeing him alive afterward; but the ascension could only be confirmed by their seeing it, and so it was arranged that Christ should ascend in open day, visibly, while they beheld — the ascension established by their eyewitness. The witnesses observe the cloud: "a cloud received him out of their sight" — the cloud, often the token of the divine presence and glory (the Shekinah, the cloud of the transfiguration), received Christ, veiling him from their eyes as he passed into heaven; the cloud both the chariot of his ascending and the screen that hid the glory beyond. The expositors draw the lesson on the bodily ascension: Christ ascended in his risen body, truly and visibly taken up to heaven — not merely in spirit, but bodily, the same body that rose; the foundation of the truth that he is now bodily in heaven, our forerunner and High Priest. The witnesses note the attestation this gave: the apostles, who would preach the ascended Christ, had seen him ascend; their testimony to his exaltation grounded in their own sight, as their testimony to the resurrection in their seeing him alive. The expositors observe the significance of the moment: the close of Christ's bodily presence on earth, the beginning of his heavenly reign; the disciples watching their Lord pass into glory, soon to receive the Spirit he would send from the Father. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rests on the attested ascension of Christ, seen by the apostles, and rejoices in the Lord bodily exalted to heaven, whence he reigns and intercedes. The verse thus records the visible ascension — Christ taken up while they beheld, a cloud receiving him — teaching the importance of the attested ascension, the bodily return of Christ to heaven, and the foundation of his heavenly reign.
10:
"And while they looked stedfastly toward heaven as he went up, behold, two men stood by them in white apparel;" The witnesses note the disciples' steadfast gaze and the appearance of the angels. Barnes explains "looked steadfastly" — they "fixed their eyes, or gazed intently toward heaven" (the same word as Luke 4:20), the intense gaze of those deeply interested; mingled with "amazement, and disappointment, and curiosity, and the earnest desire to catch the last glimpse of their beloved Master." Gill notes they kept looking upward after the cloud had hidden him. The witnesses note the "two men in white apparel" — angels — appearing to the gazing disciples. The witnesses note the angels sent to the wondering apostles.

The witnesses note the disciples gazing up after their vanished Lord, and the two angels who appeared to them.

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' gaze and the angels' appearance. The lesson the expositors draw is the longing of the disciples after their ascended Lord, and the grace of God in sending the angels to instruct them. The witnesses note the gaze: "they looked stedfastly toward heaven as he went up" — the apostles fixed their eyes intently on the sky, gazing after Christ even when the cloud had received him; Barnes notes the intense, earnest look of those deeply moved, mingling amazement at the wonder, disappointment perhaps that he had gone, curiosity, and the longing to catch a last glimpse of their beloved Master. The expositors observe the tenderness of the scene: the disciples, bereft of Christ's bodily presence, could not take their eyes from the place of his ascending; their love holding them there, gazing up, reluctant to lose sight of him. The witnesses note the appearance: "two men stood by them in white apparel" — two angels, in the white that betokens their heavenly nature and purity, suddenly present beside the gazing disciples; sent from God to instruct and comfort them. The expositors draw the lesson on the ministry of angels: as angels attended Christ's birth, his temptation, his agony, and his resurrection, so now they attend his ascension — present at every great moment of his work; and here sent to the disciples, to turn them from vain gazing to their duty and to assure them of his return. The witnesses press the grace of God in the angels' coming: the disciples, left gazing in amazement, are not abandoned, but visited by heavenly messengers — God's care providing instruction and comfort in their bewilderment. The expositors note the white apparel: the token of the angels' holiness and heavenly origin, and (as at the resurrection, Luke 24:4) the sign that these were no mere men but messengers from heaven. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the love that gazes after Christ, and the grace that sends instruction in our bewilderment; and is to be turned, like the disciples, from vain longing to faithful duty. The verse thus records the disciples' gaze and the angels' appearance — looking steadfastly, the two men in white — teaching the longing of the disciples after their ascended Lord, and the grace of God in sending the angels to instruct them.
11:
"Which also said, Ye men of Galilee, why stand ye gazing up into heaven? this same Jesus, which is taken up from you into heaven, shall so come in like manner as ye have seen him go into heaven." The witnesses note the angels' gentle reproof and the promise of the second coming. Barnes finds "a slight degree of censure" in the question — calling them from "a vain attempt to see the departed Saviour"; the impropriety being their lingering disappointment and perhaps the expectation that he would soon reappear. Gill notes "ye men of Galilee" was no reproach but a token of recognition and of distinguishing grace. The witnesses note the great promise: "this same Jesus shall so come in like manner" — the second coming, bodily, visibly, in clouds, as he ascended. The witnesses note the promise of Christ's return.

The witnesses note the angels' word: a gentle reproof of vain gazing, and the promise that Christ will so return.

So the witnesses make the verse the angels' reproof and the promise of the second coming. The lesson the expositors draw is the call from vain gazing to faithful waiting, and the great promise that Christ will return as he ascended. The witnesses note the address: "Ye men of Galilee" — Gill observes this was no reproach of their lowly origin, but a token that the angels knew them, and a reminder of the distinguishing grace that chose such humble men to be Christ's apostles. The expositors note the gentle reproof in the question: "why stand ye gazing up into heaven?" — Barnes finds a slight censure, calling them from a vain attempt to see the departed Saviour; their lingering gaze, however loving, was now useless, for Christ was gone, and they had work to do; the angels turn them from idle longing to their duty. The witnesses press the great promise: "this same Jesus, which is taken up from you into heaven, shall so come in like manner as ye have seen him go" — the assurance of the second coming; the very same Jesus, in his risen body, will return; and "in like manner" as he ascended — bodily, visibly, in the clouds, personally. The expositors draw the lesson on the manner of the return: as Christ ascended bodily, visibly, with a cloud, so he will come again — not merely spiritually or invisibly, but in person, in his body, in the clouds of heaven, every eye seeing him (Revelation 1:7); the ascension the pattern of the return. The witnesses observe the comfort in the promise: the disciples, sorrowing at his departure, are assured he will come again; the ascension no final parting, but a temporary one, ended by his glorious return; the gazing turned to hope. The expositors note the redirection: the angels turn the disciples from gazing after the ascended Christ to waiting for the returning Christ — from idle longing to active hope and faithful service in the interval; the right response to the ascension being not to gaze but to work and watch. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is called from vain longing to faithful waiting, assured that the same Jesus who ascended will so return; and is to live in the hope of his bodily, glorious coming. The verse thus records the angels' reproof and promise — why stand ye gazing? this same Jesus shall so come — teaching the call from vain gazing to faithful hope, and the great promise of Christ's bodily return as he ascended.
12:
"Then returned they unto Jerusalem from the mount called Olivet, which is from Jerusalem a sabbath day's journey." The witnesses note the return from Olivet and the note of distance. Chrysostom observes the disciples could return in peace only because the angel had "referred them to another Coming" — the promise of the return enabling them to bear the parting; and he infers from the mention of "a sabbath day's journey" that "it was also on a sabbath-day that these things took place." The witnesses note the apostles returning to Jerusalem from the Mount of Olives, the distance (about three-quarters of a mile) being a "sabbath day's journey." The witnesses note the obedient return to wait at Jerusalem.

The witnesses note the disciples returning to Jerusalem from Olivet — obeying the charge to wait, comforted by the promise.

So the witnesses make the verse the return from Olivet. The lesson the expositors draw is the obedience of the disciples in returning to wait, and the comfort of the promise that enabled them to bear the parting. The witnesses note the return: "then returned they unto Jerusalem from the mount called Olivet" — the apostles, the ascension over and the angels' word heard, returned to the city, obeying Christ's charge not to depart from Jerusalem but to wait for the Spirit (v.4). The expositors observe Chrysostom's insight on what enabled the return: the disciples could return in peace, could bear the loss of Christ's bodily presence, only because the angel had referred them to another coming — the promise of his return (v.11) consoling them; without that hope, they could not have borne the parting, but with it, they returned in faith and expectation. The witnesses note the geographical detail: "from Jerusalem a sabbath day's journey" — the distance from the city to Olivet, about three-quarters of a mile (the limit a Jew might travel on the sabbath, by rabbinic reckoning); a small note of historical exactness, and (Chrysostom infers) a hint that the ascension occurred on a sabbath. The expositors draw the lesson on obedient waiting: the disciples did not scatter to their homes or begin their work, but returned to Jerusalem as commanded, to wait for the promised Spirit; their obedience the pattern of those who wait on God for his enabling before they act. The witnesses press the comfort that sustained them: the promise of Christ's return, given by the angels, turned their sorrow to hope, and enabled them to return and wait with patience; the hope of his coming the strength of the waiting Church. The expositors observe the exactness of Luke's account: the precise note of the distance, the named mount, the historical detail — the marks of a careful, truthful history, grounding the great events in real time and place. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to obey the charge to wait on God, sustained by the hope of Christ's return; and to mark the historical exactness that confirms the truth of the account. The verse thus records the return from Olivet — a sabbath day's journey to Jerusalem — teaching the obedient return of the disciples to wait, and the comfort of the promise that enabled them to bear the parting.
13:
"And when they were come in, they went up into an upper room, where abode both Peter, and James, and John, and Andrew, Philip, and Thomas, Bartholomew, and Matthew, James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon Zelotes, and Judas the brother of James." The witnesses note the upper room and the naming of the eleven. Chrysostom notes the order of the names: "no longer is only the latter [John] together with his brother mentioned, but together with Peter the two" — Peter, James, and John now the leading three; and "he has done well to mention the disciples, for since one had betrayed" Christ, it was fitting to show the faithful eleven. Gill discusses where the upper room was (a private house, or the temple). The witnesses note the eleven apostles gathered in the upper room. The witnesses note the faithful apostles named.

The witnesses note the eleven gathered in the upper room — the faithful apostles named after the traitor's fall.

So the witnesses make the verse the gathering of the eleven in the upper room. The lesson the expositors draw is the faithful perseverance of the eleven, named after the traitor's fall, and their gathering to wait for the Spirit. The witnesses note the place: "they went up into an upper room, where abode" the apostles — an upper chamber where they lodged and gathered (Gill discusses whether a private house or a part of the temple); the place of their waiting and prayer in the days before Pentecost. The expositors observe the careful naming of the eleven, which Chrysostom marks: Luke lists the apostles, and Chrysostom notes the order — Peter, James, and John now together as the leading three (where before John was named with his brother); the inner circle prominent. The witnesses press Chrysostom's point on the purpose of the listing: "he has done well to mention the disciples, for since one had betrayed" Christ, it was fitting to record the faithful eleven who remained — distinguishing those who persevered from the traitor who fell; the roll of the faithful set against the memory of Judas. The expositors draw the lesson on perseverance: the eleven, who had continued with Christ (despite their flight and Peter's denial, now restored), are gathered faithful in the upper room; their names recorded as the foundation of the Church, the witnesses of the resurrection, the persevering apostles. The witnesses note the contrast with Judas, soon to be discussed (v.16-20): the eleven faithful, the one fallen; the roll of the apostles a testimony to perseverance and a warning of apostasy. The expositors observe the unity of the gathering: the apostles together in one place, of one accord (the next verse), waiting and praying — the nucleus of the Church, gathered in obedience and expectation. The witnesses note the historical exactness: the named apostles, the specified room — the marks of a true account, grounding the Church's beginning in real persons and places. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the perseverance of the faithful apostles, gathered to wait and pray; and the importance of steadfast continuance, set against the warning of the traitor's fall. The verse thus records the eleven in the upper room — the faithful apostles named — teaching their perseverance after the traitor's fall, and their gathering to wait for the promised Spirit.
14:
"These all continued with one accord in prayer and supplication, with the women, and Mary the mother of Jesus, and with his brethren." The witnesses note the united, persevering prayer of the gathered company. Gill details the matter of their prayer: "for the promise of the Spirit Christ had given them reason to expect; and that they might be preserved... and be abundantly qualified for preaching... and that their hearts might be comforted, and knit together in love"; and notes they were "unanimous," "constant, and assiduous," following it "with importunity." The witnesses note the company — the apostles, the women (Mary Magdalene and others), Mary the mother of Jesus, and Christ's brethren — united in prayer. The witnesses note the praying, waiting Church.

The witnesses note the gathered company in united, persevering prayer — apostles, women, Mary, and Christ's brethren.

So the witnesses make the verse the united prayer of the waiting Church. The lesson the expositors draw is the unanimous, persevering prayer by which the Church waited for the promised Spirit. The witnesses note the manner of their waiting: "these all continued with one accord in prayer and supplication" — the gathered company did not wait idly, but in earnest, united, constant prayer; their waiting filled with supplication for the promised Spirit and for all they needed. The expositors observe the qualities of their prayer that Gill names: it was unanimous ("with one accord") — the company of one heart and mind, agreed in their petitions; constant and assiduous — they "continued," persevering in prayer day by day; and importunate — pressing their requests with earnestness, as those who would not be denied. The witnesses press the matter of their prayer: for the promise of the Spirit, which Christ had bidden them expect; for their preservation and qualification for the work; for the comfort and knitting together of their hearts in love — the prayer of the waiting Church, seeking the power and grace for the mission before them. The expositors draw the lesson on prayer as the preparation for power: the Spirit came at Pentecost upon a praying Church; the disciples prepared for the outpouring not by their own efforts but by united, persevering prayer — teaching that the power of God is sought and received through prayer. The witnesses note the company gathered: the apostles, the faithful women (who had followed Christ from Galilee, stood at the cross, and come to the tomb), Mary the mother of Jesus (here last mentioned in Scripture, among the praying disciples, not exalted above them), and Christ's brethren (now believers, where once they had doubted, John 7:5) — the whole company united in prayer. The expositors observe the place of Mary: she is named among the praying disciples, one of the company, seeking the Spirit like the rest — not an object of worship, but a worshipper; the mother of the Lord here a member of the praying Church. The witnesses draw the application: the believer learns to wait on God in united, persevering, importunate prayer, especially for the Spirit's power; and to mark the praying Church as the company on which the power descends. The verse thus records the united prayer of the waiting Church — with one accord in prayer and supplication — teaching the unanimous, persevering prayer by which the disciples prepared for the promised Spirit.
15:
"And in those days Peter stood up in the midst of the disciples, and said, (the number of names together were about an hundred and twenty,)" The witnesses note Peter's leadership and the number of the disciples. Gill notes Peter rose "not only as a forward person, and who had been used to be the first mover... but as willing to show his zeal for Christ, whom he had so lately denied, and as being the senior man in company, as well as the minister of the circumcision." Matthew Henry notes the sin of Judas "made a vacancy in the college of the apostles," ordained twelve with an eye to the twelve tribes. The witnesses note Peter, in the days of waiting, standing up among the hundred and twenty to propose filling Judas's place. The witnesses note Peter's leadership of the gathered disciples.

The witnesses note Peter rising to lead — the restored disciple, zealous for Christ, addressing the hundred and twenty.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's leadership among the disciples. The lesson the expositors draw is the restored Peter taking his place of leadership, and the company of believers gathered to fill the gap left by Judas. The witnesses note the act: "Peter stood up in the midst of the disciples" — taking the lead, rising to address the company and propose the filling of Judas's place; the forward, active disciple resuming his place. The expositors observe Gill's account of why Peter led: not only because he was naturally forward, the first mover (as so often in the Gospels), but because he was zealous for Christ, whom he had so lately denied — eager to show his restored devotion; and because he was the senior in the company, and (as later) the apostle of the circumcision; his leadership fitting and restored. The witnesses press the restoration of Peter: the disciple who had denied his Lord, wept bitterly, and been forgiven and recommissioned (John 21), now stands forth as a leader — the fallen restored to honour and usefulness; the grace of Christ not only pardoning but reinstating. The expositors note the number: "about an hundred and twenty" — the company of believers gathered in Jerusalem; a small beginning, yet the nucleus of the Church soon to be multiplied at Pentecost; the named apostles and the wider company of disciples. The witnesses observe Matthew Henry's point on the occasion: the sin of Judas had made a vacancy in the apostolic college, which was ordained twelve, answering to the twelve tribes of Israel; and it was fitting the number be restored, the gap filled, before the great outpouring. The expositors draw the lesson on the restoration of order: the Church, gathered and waiting, sees to the filling of the apostolic vacancy; the number twelve, with its symbolic completeness, to be made up before Pentecost. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the grace that restored Peter to leadership, and the orderly care of the Church in filling the place left by the traitor; the fallen restored, the order maintained. The verse thus records Peter's leadership among the hundred and twenty — standing up to propose the filling of Judas's place — teaching the restoration of Peter and the gathered company's care to make up the apostolic number.
16:
"Men and brethren, this scripture must needs have been fulfilled, which the Holy Ghost by the mouth of David spake before concerning Judas, which was guide to them that took Jesus." The witnesses note Peter's appeal to the fulfilment of prophecy concerning Judas. Barnes identifies the address "Men and brethren" as "a customary mode of address, implying affection and respect," and notes the prophecy (Psalm 41:9, "mine own familiar friend... hath lifted up his heel against me," applied to Judas by Christ, John 13:18). The witnesses note Peter teaching that the betrayal and fall of Judas were foretold by the Holy Ghost through David, and "must needs" be fulfilled — the Scripture certain. The witnesses note the divine inspiration of Scripture and its sure fulfilment.

The witnesses note Peter's appeal: the Scripture, spoken by the Holy Ghost through David, must be fulfilled concerning Judas.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's appeal to fulfilled prophecy. The lesson the expositors draw is the divine inspiration of Scripture, its certain fulfilment, and the foretelling of Judas's betrayal. The witnesses note Peter's address: "Men and brethren" — Barnes notes the customary form, implying affection and respect (better rendered "Men, our brethren"); Peter addresses the gathered company as fellow-believers. The expositors observe the substance of his argument: "this scripture must needs have been fulfilled, which the Holy Ghost by the mouth of David spake before concerning Judas" — Peter teaches that the betrayal and fall of Judas were foretold in Scripture, and that the prophecy "must needs" be fulfilled; the certainty of God's word. The witnesses press the doctrine of inspiration in Peter's words: "the Holy Ghost by the mouth of David spake" — the Scriptures are the word of the Holy Ghost, spoken through the human authors; David the mouth, the Spirit the speaker; the Old Testament Scriptures divinely inspired, their predictions sure (2 Peter 1:21). The expositors draw the lesson on the fulfilment of prophecy in Judas: the psalm of the betrayed sufferer (Psalm 41:9, applied by Christ to Judas, John 13:18) foretold the treachery of the familiar friend; and Judas, "guide to them that took Jesus," fulfilled it — the betrayal no surprise to God, but foretold and certain. The witnesses observe the providence and the responsibility joined: that Judas's betrayal was foretold did not lessen his guilt (as Christ said, "woe to that man," Luke 22:22); the prophecy declared what the traitor would freely and wickedly do, God foreseeing and overruling his sin. The expositors note the use Peter makes of the prophecy: it both explains the fall of Judas (foretold, not a failure of Christ's choosing) and grounds the filling of his place (also foretold, v.20); the Scripture guiding the Church's action. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rests on the inspiration and certain fulfilment of Scripture, sees the betrayal of Judas as foretold, and marks the providence that overruled even the traitor's sin to fulfil the word. The verse thus records Peter's appeal to fulfilled prophecy — the Holy Ghost by David spake concerning Judas — teaching the inspiration of Scripture, the certainty of its fulfilment, and the foretelling of the traitor's betrayal.
17:
"For he was numbered with us, and had obtained part of this ministry." The witnesses note Judas's place among the apostles and the warning it gives. Barnes presses the sobering truth: "this does not mean that he was a true Christian, but that he was reckoned among the apostles; Jesus knew that he never loved him... he declared that he was a devil (John 6:70); he knew his whole character when he chose him." The witnesses note that Judas had been numbered among the twelve and had shared in the apostolic ministry — yet was a false disciple, a traitor; the warning that one may hold the highest office and yet be lost. The witnesses note the false apostle among the chosen.

The witnesses note Judas's place among the apostles — numbered with them, sharing the ministry, yet a false disciple.

So the witnesses make the verse Judas's place among the apostles. The lesson the expositors draw is the sobering truth that Judas held the highest office and shared the apostolic ministry, yet was a false disciple — a warning against false profession. The witnesses note the fact: "he was numbered with us, and had obtained part of this ministry" — Judas had been reckoned among the twelve, ordained an apostle with the rest, and had shared in the apostolic work; outwardly one of the chosen, holding the highest office in the Church. The expositors press the sobering distinction Barnes draws: this does not mean Judas was a true Christian, but only that he was reckoned among the apostles; Jesus knew he never loved him, had declared him a devil (John 6:70), and knew his whole character when he chose him — Judas a false disciple from the first, an apostle in office but a traitor in heart. The witnesses observe the warning this gives: a man may hold the highest place in the Church, share its ministry, work its miracles, and yet be a false disciple, unconverted, lost — the office no proof of grace, the outward calling no guarantee of the heart. The expositors draw the lesson on false profession: Judas the great warning that one may be numbered among the apostles, trusted, honoured, and yet be a hypocrite and a traitor; "not every one that saith Lord, Lord" (Matthew 7:21); the need to examine whether our profession is matched by a true heart. The witnesses note the providence in Christ's choosing of Judas: knowing his character, Christ yet chose him — that the Scriptures might be fulfilled, and (some observe) that the Church might learn that false disciples may be among the truest fellowship, and that the betrayal of Christ should come from within, fulfilling the prophecy of the familiar friend. The expositors observe the dreadful contrast: Judas had every privilege — the company of Christ, the apostolic office, the sight of the miracles — yet fell, betrayed his Lord, and perished; the highest privilege abused ending in the deepest ruin. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is warned by Judas against false profession, called to examine his heart, and reminded that office and privilege are no security without true grace. The verse thus records Judas's place among the apostles — numbered with them, sharing the ministry — teaching the sobering warning that the highest office may be held by a false disciple, and the peril of profession without grace.
18:
"Now this man purchased a field with the reward of iniquity; and falling headlong, he burst asunder in the midst, and all his bowels gushed out." The witnesses note the end of Judas and the harmonizing with Matthew. Gill treats verses 18-19 as "the words of Luke the historian," a parenthesis; and addresses the apparent difficulty that Judas is said to "purchase" the field, while Matthew says the chief priests bought it (Matthew 27:7) — "both are true; Judas having received his money... the priests buying it with his money," in his name or with his returned wages. The witnesses note the manner of Judas's death — falling headlong, bursting asunder — harmonized with Matthew's "hanged himself" (the rope or branch breaking, or the body falling after). The witnesses note the dreadful end of the traitor.

The witnesses note the dreadful end of Judas — the field of blood and his fall — harmonized with Matthew's account.

So the witnesses make the verse the dreadful end of Judas. The lesson the expositors draw is the fearful judgment on the traitor, and the harmony of the two accounts of his death and the field. The witnesses note the parenthesis: verses 18-19 are (Gill argues) the words of Luke the historian, not part of Peter's speech — an explanatory note for the reader about Judas's end and the field; for Peter's hearers, on the spot, would not need to be told what all Jerusalem knew. The expositors address the two apparent difficulties. First, the purchase of the field: Acts says Judas "purchased a field with the reward of iniquity," while Matthew says the chief priests bought it (Matthew 27:7); the harmony, Gill notes, is that both are true — Judas's money (the thirty pieces he returned) bought the field, the priests buying it with his wages, in his name or on his account; so it was, in effect, his purchase, though their act. Second, the manner of his death: Acts says he fell headlong and burst asunder, while Matthew says he hanged himself (Matthew 27:5); the harmony is that Judas hanged himself, and then (the rope or branch breaking, or the body falling) fell headlong and burst — the two accounts describing different stages of the one dreadful death. The witnesses press the fearful judgment displayed: the traitor's end was horrible — the bursting body, the gushing bowels; a death of shame and horror, the visible mark of God's judgment on so great a sin. The expositors draw the lesson on the wages of betrayal: Judas bought a field with the price of his Lord's blood, and died a dreadful death upon it; "the reward of iniquity" issuing in destruction; the traitor's end a warning of the fearful judgment on those who sell Christ for gain. The witnesses note the providence in the field of blood: the very money of the betrayal became the field of the traitor's burial and shame, a perpetual memorial of his crime (the next verse). The expositors observe the honest handling of the apparent discrepancies: the witnesses do not hide the difficulty, but show the harmony — the accounts supplementing, not contradicting, one another. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the fearful end of the traitor, the wages of betraying Christ, and the harmony of the faithful record. The verse thus records the dreadful end of Judas — the field of blood, the headlong fall — teaching the fearful judgment on the traitor and the harmony of the two accounts of his death.
19:
"And it was known unto all the dwellers at Jerusalem; insomuch as that field is called in their proper tongue, Aceldama, that is to say, The field of blood." The witnesses note the field of blood and its public memorial of Judas's crime. Barnes explains "Aceldama" as "composed of two Syro-Chaldaic words, and means literally, the field of blood"; the name preserving "the memory of the guilt of Judas." Clarke notes that the whole affair — "the repentance of Judas, his dying testimony in behalf of our Lord's innocence, and his tragical death" — was publicly known in Jerusalem. The witnesses note the field bought with the betrayal-money, called the field of blood, a standing memorial of the traitor's deed. The witnesses note the public testimony of Judas's crime.

The witnesses note the field of blood — a public memorial of Judas's betrayal, known to all Jerusalem.

So the witnesses make the verse the field of blood as a public memorial. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence that made the traitor's crime a standing, public testimony, and the confirmation it gave to the gospel facts. The witnesses note the public knowledge: "it was known unto all the dwellers at Jerusalem" — the whole affair of Judas's betrayal, his returning the money, his dreadful death, and the purchase of the field, was widely known in the city; no secret matter, but a public fact, attested by all. The expositors observe the name and its meaning: the field was called "Aceldama," which Barnes explains as Syro-Chaldaic for "the field of blood" — so named because bought with the price of innocent blood (Matthew 27:8), and perhaps because of Judas's bloody death upon it; the name a perpetual memorial, preserving the memory of the traitor's guilt. The witnesses press the providence in this memorial: the very money of the betrayal became a field bearing a name that, to all generations, proclaimed the crime — the betrayal of innocent blood; the traitor's sin written, as it were, on the soil of Jerusalem, a standing witness against him. The expositors draw the lesson on the confirmation of the gospel: the public knowledge of these facts — the field, the name, the death — confirmed the truth of the betrayal and the innocence of Christ; the very enemies of the gospel could not deny what all Jerusalem knew; the field of blood a silent witness to the events of the Passion. The witnesses note Clarke's observation that even Judas's dying testimony to Christ's innocence ("I have betrayed innocent blood," Matthew 27:4) was publicly known — the traitor himself, in his remorse, bearing witness to the guiltlessness of the Lord he sold. The expositors observe the warning in the memorial: the field of blood a perpetual sermon on the wages of betraying Christ — the traitor's name and crime preserved, his sin made a public byword; a warning to all who would sell Christ for gain. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the providence that turned the traitor's crime into a public testimony, confirming the gospel facts and warning against the love of money that betrayed the Lord. The verse thus records the field of blood — Aceldama, known to all Jerusalem — teaching the providence that made the traitor's crime a public memorial, confirming the gospel and warning against betrayal.
20:
"For it is written in the book of Psalms, Let his habitation be desolate, and let no man dwell therein: and his bishoprick let another take." The witnesses note Peter's two-fold citation from the Psalms. Barnes identifies the first from Psalm 69:25 ("let their habitation be desolate") and notes the second from Psalm 109:8 ("let another take his office"); the plural changed to the singular and applied to Judas. The witnesses note Peter grounding the filling of Judas's place on Scripture — the first psalm foretelling the desolation of the traitor, the second the transfer of his office ("bishoprick," his apostolic charge) to another. The witnesses note the scriptural warrant for replacing Judas.

The witnesses note Peter's scriptural warrant: the Psalms foretold Judas's desolation and the passing of his office to another.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's scriptural warrant for filling Judas's place. The lesson the expositors draw is that the fall of Judas and the transfer of his office were both foretold in Scripture, grounding the Church's action. The witnesses note the two citations Peter makes from the Psalms. The first, "Let his habitation be desolate, and let no man dwell therein" — from Psalm 69:25 (Barnes notes), a psalm of the suffering righteous one whose enemies are cursed; applied to Judas, foretelling the desolation of the traitor, his dwelling made empty, himself cut off. The second, "his bishoprick let another take" — from Psalm 109:8 ("let another take his office"), foretelling that the traitor's office would pass to another; applied to the apostolic charge of Judas, which another must now fill. The expositors observe the change Barnes notes from plural to singular: the psalms speak of enemies in the plural, but Peter applies them to the one traitor, Judas, by the Spirit's guidance — the prophetic words finding their fulfilment in him. The witnesses press the use Peter makes of the second citation: "his bishoprick let another take" — the word "bishoprick" (episkopē, oversight, office) here the apostolic charge of Judas; and Peter draws from it the warrant that another must be chosen to take the fallen apostle's place, the office not to be left vacant but filled. The expositors draw the lesson on the scriptural grounding of the Church's action: Peter does not act on his own authority or mere expediency, but on the warrant of Scripture — the Psalms foretelling both the desolation of the traitor and the transfer of his office; the Church's filling of the vacancy grounded in the prophetic word. The witnesses note the providence in the prophecy: the fall of Judas and the choosing of his successor were not afterthoughts, but foretold in the Psalms; God's purpose embracing even the traitor's fall and the restoration of the apostolic number. The expositors observe the doctrine of office implied: the apostolic charge is a "bishoprick," an office of oversight, which passes (where vacated by death or fall) to a successor; the Church maintaining its appointed order by the filling of vacant offices. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the scriptural warrant for the Church's action, the foretelling of Judas's fall and replacement, and the providence that ordered even these things. The verse thus records Peter's two-fold citation — the desolation of the traitor and the transfer of his office — teaching the scriptural grounding of the filling of Judas's place, foretold in the Psalms.
21:
"Wherefore of these men which have companied with us all the time that the Lord Jesus went in and out among us," The witnesses note the qualification for the apostleship — long companionship with Christ. Gill draws out the requirements: the choice should be of "one among the men" (not the women), and "of one from among themselves, and who was known unto them; with whose abilities, integrity, wisdom, and holy conversation they were acquainted" — "one of our own company, and not a stranger." The witnesses note the first qualification: the candidate must have companied with the apostles throughout Christ's earthly ministry, an eyewitness of all he did. The witnesses note the qualification of long companionship.

The witnesses note the first qualification: one who had companied with the apostles throughout Christ's ministry.

So the witnesses make the verse the qualification of long companionship for the apostleship. The lesson the expositors draw is that the apostle must be a witness who had accompanied Christ throughout his ministry, known and tried among the disciples. The witnesses note the qualification Peter sets: "of these men which have companied with us all the time that the Lord Jesus went in and out among us" — the candidate must be one who had been a constant companion of the apostles and of Christ, present throughout the Lord's earthly ministry, from its beginning to the ascension; an eyewitness of all that Jesus did and taught. The expositors observe the reason for this requirement: the apostle's office was to be a witness of Christ — especially of his resurrection (the next verse) — and so he must be one who had seen and known Christ, accompanied him, and could testify from personal knowledge; not a stranger or a recent comer, but a tried and intimate companion. The witnesses press Gill's drawing-out of the requirements: the choice was to be "of these men" — among the men, not the women, the apostolic office being for men; and of one "from among themselves," known to the company, "with whose abilities, integrity, wisdom, and holy conversation they were acquainted" — a tested and known character, not an untried stranger. The expositors draw the lesson on the qualification of witnesses: the foundation of the gospel rests on the testimony of those who had companied with Christ and seen him risen; the apostle a credible eyewitness, his testimony grounded in long and intimate knowledge of the Lord. The witnesses note the wisdom of choosing a known and tried man: the Church does not entrust the high office to an unknown or untested person, but to one whose character and faith had been proved by long companionship; a rule of prudence for the choice of those who bear office. The expositors observe the dignity of the qualification: to have companied with Christ throughout his ministry was a high privilege and a necessary qualification for the apostleship; the apostle one who had walked with the Lord. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the qualification of the apostolic witness — long companionship with Christ — and the prudence of the Church in choosing tried and known men for office. The verse thus records the first qualification — companionship with the apostles throughout Christ's ministry — teaching that the apostle must be a tried eyewitness, known and proved among the disciples.
22:
"Beginning from the baptism of John, unto that same day that he was taken up from us, must one be ordained to be a witness with us of his resurrection." The witnesses note the full span of required witness and the apostle's chief office. Clarke notes "beginning from the baptism of John" — "from the time that Christ was baptized by John in Jordan, for it was at that time that his public ministry properly began." Gill stresses the candidate must have followed Christ from that beginning, his "faith, love, and firmness of mind... sufficiently tried." The witnesses note the apostle must witness the whole of Christ's ministry, from his baptism to his ascension, and above all be "a witness with us of his resurrection" — the central testimony. The witnesses note the apostle as witness of the resurrection.

The witnesses note the apostle's span and office: witness from Christ's baptism to his ascension, chiefly of his resurrection.

So the witnesses make the verse the full span of witness and the apostle's chief office. The lesson the expositors draw is that the apostle must have witnessed Christ's whole ministry, and above all be a witness of his resurrection. The witnesses note the span Peter sets: "beginning from the baptism of John, unto that same day that he was taken up from us" — the candidate must have accompanied Christ from the start of his public ministry (his baptism by John, when, Clarke notes, his ministry properly began) to its close (the ascension); a witness of the whole course of Christ's earthly work. The expositors observe the completeness this required: the apostle must have seen and known Christ throughout — his teaching, his miracles, his death, his resurrection, his ascension; a full and continuous eyewitness, able to testify of all. The witnesses press the chief office named: "must one be ordained to be a witness with us of his resurrection" — the central testimony of the apostle is to the resurrection of Christ; the apostle above all a witness that Jesus is risen, having seen him alive after his death. The expositors draw the lesson on the resurrection as the heart of the apostolic testimony: the apostles were chosen and ordained to bear witness to the risen Christ; the resurrection the central fact of the gospel, and the apostle its appointed witness — "this Jesus hath God raised up, whereof we all are witnesses" (Acts 2:32). The witnesses note the qualification this implies: the candidate must have seen the risen Lord, able to testify from his own sight; the apostolic witness grounded in the personal seeing of the risen Christ. The expositors observe the dignity and burden of the office: to be a witness of the resurrection is the high calling of the apostle, the foundation of the gospel resting on this testimony; the apostles staking their lives on what they had seen. The witnesses note the word "ordained" — the candidate to be appointed, set apart to this office; the Church ordaining the witness to fill the vacant place. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the apostle's office as witness of the resurrection, the central testimony of the gospel; and rests his faith on the attested witness of those who saw the risen Lord. The verse thus records the full span of witness and the apostle's chief office — from John's baptism to the ascension, a witness of the resurrection — teaching that the apostle must have known Christ's whole ministry and above all testify of his rising.
23:
"And they appointed two, Joseph called Barsabas, who was surnamed Justus, and Matthias." The witnesses note the nomination of two candidates. Gill notes Peter's motion "was attended to by the whole company; they approved of it, and accordingly proposed two persons by name; one of which was to be chosen, not by the apostles, but by the whole assembly" — "the whole body of the people were concerned in this affair." Lightfoot discusses the name "Joseph called Barsabas" (Jose and Joseph being one name among the Jews). The witnesses note the company nominating two qualified men — Joseph Barsabas (surnamed Justus) and Matthias — from whom one would be chosen to fill Judas's place. The witnesses note the two candidates put forward.

The witnesses note the two candidates nominated — Joseph Barsabas and Matthias — by the whole company.

So the witnesses make the verse the nomination of the two candidates. The lesson the expositors draw is the orderly, communal process of the choice, the whole company concerned in filling the apostolic vacancy. The witnesses note the action: "they appointed two" — the company, having approved Peter's motion, nominated two men, both qualified by the requirements (long companionship, witness of the resurrection); from these two, one would be chosen to fill Judas's place. The expositors observe Gill's point on the communal nature of the choice: the nomination was not Peter's alone, nor the apostles' only, but the act of "the whole company" — they approved, proposed, and (the verses to come) prayed and cast lots; the whole body of disciples concerned in the choice of the new apostle. The witnesses press the orderliness of the process: the candidates named, the qualifications met, the choice to be made by prayer and the lot — a careful, reverent, communal proceeding, not a hasty or arbitrary appointment; the Church acting in order and with deliberation. The expositors note the two men: "Joseph called Barsabas, who was surnamed Justus, and Matthias" — Joseph Barsabas (Lightfoot notes Jose and Joseph being one name), surnamed Justus (the just, perhaps a name of esteem), and Matthias; two faithful disciples, both fit for the office, both having companied with Christ and witnessed his resurrection. The witnesses observe that two were named, though only one was needed — the company narrowing the choice to two qualified men, then leaving the final decision to God (by the lot); human nomination and divine choice combined. The expositors draw the lesson on the right way of filling office: the Church proposes qualified candidates, then seeks God's direction in the choice; the process orderly, prayerful, and dependent on God. The witnesses note the honour of both candidates: both were worthy, both qualified; the one not chosen (Joseph Barsabas) no less faithful, but God's choice falling on Matthias. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the orderly, communal, prayerful process of the choice, and the combining of human nomination with dependence on God's direction. The verse thus records the nomination of the two candidates — Joseph Barsabas and Matthias — teaching the orderly, communal process of filling the apostolic vacancy, the whole company concerned in the choice.
24:
"And they prayed, and said, Thou, Lord, which knowest the hearts of all men, shew whether of these two thou hast chosen," The witnesses note the prayer for divine direction and the address to the heart-knower. Barnes notes the example: "as they could not agree on the individual, they invoked the direction of God in their choice — an example which should be followed in every selection of an individual to exercise the duties of the sacred office." On "which knowest the hearts of all men," Barnes observes this is "the peculiar prerogative of God" (Jeremiah 17:10), "yet this attribute is also expressly ascribed to Jesus Christ" (Revelation 2:23; John 2:25) — so the prayer may be addressed to Christ. The witnesses note the disciples praying to the heart-knower to show his choice. The witnesses note the prayer for guidance in choosing.

The witnesses note the prayer to the heart-knowing Lord — to reveal which of the two he had chosen.

So the witnesses make the verse the prayer for divine direction. The lesson the expositors draw is the dependence on God in the choice of office, and the address to the Lord who knows all hearts. The witnesses note the prayer: "they prayed, and said, Thou, Lord, which knowest the hearts of all men, shew whether of these two thou hast chosen" — unable to decide between two equally qualified men, the disciples sought God's direction, asking him to reveal his choice; the choice referred to God who alone knows the hearts. The expositors observe the example Barnes draws: the disciples, unable to agree, invoked God's direction — an example for every choice of those who bear sacred office; the Church to seek God's guidance, by prayer, in the appointment of its ministers and officers. The witnesses press the ground of the appeal: "which knowest the hearts of all men" — God alone knows the heart, sees what man cannot, discerns the true character and fitness hidden from human sight; and so he is appealed to, to choose between the two whose hearts he alone fully knew. The expositors note the question Barnes raises about the address: the knowledge of all hearts is the peculiar prerogative of God (Jeremiah 17:10; 1 Chronicles 28:9); yet this very attribute is expressly ascribed to Jesus Christ in Scripture (Revelation 2:23, "I am he which searcheth the reins and hearts"; John 2:25) — so the prayer, addressed to the heart-knowing "Lord," may well be addressed to Christ, who knows all hearts and who had chosen the original apostles. The witnesses draw the lesson on the deity of Christ implied: if the prayer is to Christ (as the searcher of hearts, and the one who chose the apostles), it ascribes to him the divine attribute of omniscience — a witness to his Godhead; the heart-knowing Lord being divine. The expositors observe the fitness of referring the choice to the heart-knower: man judges by the outward appearance, but God looks on the heart (1 Samuel 16:7); and the choice of an apostle, requiring the discernment of the heart, was rightly left to him who knows all hearts. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to seek God's direction in choices, especially of office, depending on the Lord who knows all hearts; and to mark the divine omniscience ascribed to the heart-knowing Lord. The verse thus records the prayer for divine direction — Thou which knowest all hearts, shew thy choice — teaching dependence on God in the choice of office, and the address to the heart-knowing Lord, who alone discerns the heart.
25:
"That he may take part of this ministry and apostleship, from which Judas by transgression fell, that he might go to his own place." The witnesses note the office to be filled and the dreadful phrase "his own place."
