Gill notes the ministry and apostleship — "preaching the Gospel, administering ordinances, planting churches, and working miracles" — the part Judas had and lost. Lightfoot expounds "his own place" by the rabbinic usage: as it was said of Balaam and others that they "went to his own place, that is, into hell" — a place "appointed for idolaters" and betrayers; "where must he have his place that betrayed the very Messiah of Israel?" The witnesses note Judas's fall from the apostleship and his going to his own place — the place of the lost. The witnesses note the solemn end of the traitor.

The witnesses note the office Judas lost, and the dreadful "his own place" — the place of the lost traitor.

So the witnesses make the verse the office to be filled and the doom of Judas. The lesson the expositors draw is the high office the traitor forfeited, and the solemn truth that he went to "his own place" — the place of the lost. The witnesses note the office: "that he may take part of this ministry and apostleship, from which Judas by transgression fell" — the new apostle to take up the ministry and apostleship that Judas had held and forfeited; the office (Gill notes) of preaching the gospel, administering the ordinances, planting churches, working miracles — the apostolic charge, lost by the traitor's transgression. The expositors observe the cause of Judas's loss: "by transgression fell" — Judas fell from his office not by misfortune but by his own sin, his betrayal of Christ; the high place forfeited by his wickedness, a fall self-procured. The witnesses press the dreadful phrase "that he might go to his own place" — and Lightfoot expounds it by the rabbinic usage, in which it was said of Balaam and other wicked men that they "went to his own place, that is, into hell," the place appointed for idolaters and betrayers; so Judas, by his transgression, went to his own place — the place his sin had fitted him for, the place of the lost. The expositors draw the solemn lesson: "his own place" is the place each man's character and choices fit him for — for Judas, the betrayer of the Messiah, a place of woe; the traitor going, by his own sin, to the doom he had earned. The witnesses note the contrast with the new apostle: the one takes up the office of honour and service; the other goes to his own place of woe — the same office held by a faithful man unto glory, by the traitor unto destruction. The expositors observe the warning: every man goes at last to "his own place" — the place his life and heart have fitted him for; the believer to a place of glory with Christ, the impenitent to a place of woe; and Judas the fearful example of the traitor's doom. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the office the traitor forfeited and the place he went to, and is warned to make his calling and election sure, that his own place be with Christ, not with the lost. The verse thus records the office Judas lost and his going to his own place — teaching the high charge forfeited by transgression, and the solemn doom of the traitor in the place his sin had fitted him for.
26:
"And they gave forth their lots; and the lot fell upon Matthias; and he was numbered with the eleven apostles." The witnesses note the casting of lots and the choice of Matthias. Gill describes the method — the names written, cast into a vessel, and drawn, "no other than balloting"; or the lot of "apostle" drawn against Matthias's name; likening it to the lots cast on the day of atonement. The witnesses note the use of the lot, an Old Testament method of seeking God's decision (Proverbs 16:33), here used before Pentecost; and the result — Matthias chosen and numbered with the eleven, the apostolic twelve restored. The witnesses note the completion of the apostolic number.

The witnesses note the lot falling on Matthias — chosen by God's decision, numbered with the eleven, the twelve restored.

So the witnesses make the verse the choice of Matthias by lot. The lesson the expositors draw is the use of the lot to seek God's decision, and the restoration of the apostolic twelve. The witnesses note the method: "they gave forth their lots; and the lot fell upon Matthias" — the disciples, having prayed for God's direction, cast lots between the two candidates; Gill describes the manner (the names written, cast into a vessel, and drawn — "no other than balloting" — or the lot of "apostle" drawn against a name), likening it to the lots cast on the day of atonement (Leviticus 16:8). The expositors observe the principle of the lot: "the lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD" (Proverbs 16:33) — the lot an Old Testament method of seeking God's decision, removing the choice from human will and referring it to God; here used to discern which of the two God had chosen. The witnesses note the timing: this was before Pentecost, before the Spirit was poured out; and some observe that the lot, fitting under the old dispensation, was not used by the Church after the Spirit came, the Church thenceforth guided by the Spirit; while others defend the disciples' use of it here as a legitimate, prayerful seeking of God's will in the interim. The expositors draw the lesson, holding the disciples' action sound: having prayed to the heart-knowing Lord (v.24), they used the appointed means to receive his answer; the lot, sought in prayer and dependence, the way God then revealed his choice — Matthias chosen by God's own decision, not man's. The witnesses note the result: "he was numbered with the eleven apostles" — Matthias added to the eleven, the apostolic twelve restored, the gap left by Judas filled; the symbolic number (answering the twelve tribes) made up before the great outpouring. The expositors observe the providence: God's choice of Matthias, declared by the lot, restored the apostolic college to its appointed completeness, the foundation of the Church made whole before Pentecost. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the seeking of God's will by prayer and appointed means, and the restoration of the apostolic twelve; and trusts the God who disposes the lot and orders his Church. The verse thus records the choice of Matthias by lot — numbered with the eleven — teaching the seeking of God's decision and the restoration of the apostolic twelve before the coming of the Spirit.
